query: Our HR director has been pushing back hard on the temp-heavy summer schedule — she wants total temporary labor across the whole year held to no more than 40 man-days so temps stay a real supplement and not the workhorse. How does that change farm income?
expected_pyomo: model.tlab_annual_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.tlab[tm] for tm in model.tm) <= 40)

query: The buyer we deliver to is squeezed on storage and warehouse space this season, so there's a proposal on the table to hold our total marketed tonnage — corn, cotton, beans, sisal, everything we ship off the farm added together — to no more than 5 tons for the year. We'd consume the rest on-farm. What does the optimizer return for farm income under that ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.total_sales_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.sales[c] for c in model.c) <= 5.0)

query: Our co-op advisor keeps warning that the herd shouldn't outgrow what the fields can feed it — the head count we carry should stay tied to how much crop tonnage the farm actually grows. We're looking at writing that in as a rule: livestock head held to no more than the farm's total crop production in tons. Plug that coupling in and tell me where farm income and the herd size land.
expected_pyomo: model.herd_vs_crop = Constraint(expr=model.xlive <= sum(model.xprod[c] for c in model.c))

query: Looking at the schedule, temporary labor concentrates in peak months — over 50 man-days in July alone — while family labor stays pinned at full capacity. Couldn't we require that in every month temporary labor never makes up more than 25% of the total labor used that month (family plus temporary)? Shouldn't a smoother labor distribution beat the current plan?

query: Labor cost looks heavy relative to what the farm earns — eating roughly a third of revenue right now. Wouldn't tying labor spend to a tighter ratio, say no more than 30% of revenue, give us a leaner plan?

query: The plan leans awfully hard on a couple of crops — corn alone is moving north of three tons while half the crops barely reach the market. That kind of concentration feels exposed if any one of those markets turns. Shouldn't we hold every crop's sales to the same modest ceiling — no more than 1.5 tons each — so no single crop carries the whole income? What's keeping the model from spreading the marketing out like that?

query: The model is cramming nearly all of the good land with crops and leaving the livestock no room there. We'd like to keep some of our best ground in reserve — couldn't we hold cropping on good land to at most 6 hectares so there's slack for grazing or a fallow rotation? What's stopping the model from leaving that buffer?
expected_pyomo: model.good_crop_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.a[p] * model.xcrop[p, 'good'] for (p, s) in model.ps if s == 'good') <= 6.0)
